front: War: an 1864-1870 conflict fought between Paraguay and a namesake coalition of Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay
War: also known as the Paraguayan War, Guerra de la Triple Alianza, or Guerra Guasu
War: devastated Paraguay's male population, with clues citing estimates ranging from about 60% to over 90% killed
Event: began with Francisco Solano Lopez's invasion of the Brazilian state of Mato Grosso
Event: began when the Paraguayan gunboat Tacuari captured the Brazilian steamer Marquess of Olinda

Event: a Paraguayan counter-invasion crossing Argentina's Corrientes province prompted Bartolome Mitre to join Pedro II against Paraguay, after Paraguay's earlier intervention supporting the Colorados in Uruguay
Event: historians dispute journalist Juan O'Leary's claim that Britain covertly incited this war by opposing settlement of 900 Lincolnshire farmers in Paraguay
Event: Pelham Horton Box's book on this war rejects the "reckless dictator" theory of its causes
Event: built on a sharp horseshoe bend of the Paraguay River, defended by chain booms and contact mines called "torpedoes"
Event: defended in part by a "London battery" (named for a London recruitment firm for artillerymen) and a battery named for a Paraguayan leader's mistress
Event: nicknamed the "Gibraltar of South America"
Event: its siege delayed the Allies' advance into Paraguay by about two years
Event: Allied ironclads isolated it and a fleet of six monitors bypassed it, leading to its capture under the Duke of Caxias
Battle: Paraguay's river fleet was crushed at this naval engagement after being refused passage through Corrientes, an early turning point in the war
Battle: Francisco Solano Lopez's last major offensive of the war (May 1866); Paraguayan cavalry beat their Argentine counterparts, but his flank attack failed to overcome the Allied defenses
Battle: the Count of Eu's forces defeated a unit of roughly 3,500 Paraguayan child soldiers here
Battle: this defeat of Paraguayan child soldiers is still commemorated in Paraguay as Children's Day
Battle: a Paraguayan leader refused to surrender alongside his mistress Eliza Lynch here, reportedly shouting "I die with my nation!" before his death
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Battle: Eliza Lynch was captured here
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Event: after her 15-year-old son, Colonel Juan Francisco, was killed in this war, Eliza Lynch buried him with her bare hands and reportedly asked "Is this the civilization you have promised?"
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Event: toward this war's end, executions were carried out by lance because ammunition had grown scarce
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Event: one side's cavalry was crippled during this war by a spinal disease that killed most of its horses
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Event: allegedly stole jewels from the Virgin of Caapucu as war treasure, later lost during the war
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Event: a Paraguayan leader's brother Benigno was killed during the San Fernando Massacre in this war
work: Death of Lopez & Battle of Cerro Cora

front: Ruler: ruled Paraguay first as consul from 1841 to 1844, then as dictator until 1862
Ruler: abolished slavery in Paraguay and came to personally own half the country's land
Ruler: declared Paraguay's independence and ruled the country for 24 years as "El Supremo"
Ruler: led Paraguay into the War of the Triple Alliance via his invasion of Mato Grosso and his meddling in Uruguay
Leader: president of Argentina from 1862 to 1868 who initially commanded Allied forces before ceding command to the Duke of Caxias in 1866
Leader: commanded Brazil's troops and engineered the "Deed of December," a march through the Chaco region to flank Paraguayan defenses
Leader: took over command of Allied forces for the war's final phase
Event: U.S. President Rutherford Hayes arbitrated a postwar border dispute stemming from this war
Leader: expanded Brazilian territory at Paraguay's expense after the war and formulated a doctrine that dominated Brazilian foreign policy into the 20th century
Leader: took office as president of Argentina in this war's final days
Event: victors melted down the church bells of the defeated capital to create the "Church Cannon"
